headline: HOGS CRASH NINE PERCENT AS FEEDERS FLIP SCRIPT
subheadline: Cattle complex splits as feeder momentum turns while lean hogs crater on technical breakdown.
lead: Lean hogs crashed 8.6% Friday to $90.88, the worst single-session drop in six months as the technical floor finally gave way. Live cattle held $248.90, down just a half percent, but feeders flipped positive at $367.38, up three-tenths on the week. The cattle complex is telling two different stories now, and hogs just told theirs loud.
the_takeaway: Feeder strength with live cattle weakness means the complex is pricing different timelines.
teaser: Hogs crater while feeders turn green in a week that split the livestock story.

one_number.context: The worst single-session drop for hogs in six months, breaking through every technical level on the chart. No specific news catalyst, just pure liquidation after months of range-bound trade. When hogs move this hard this fast, it's usually fund positioning, not fundamentals.

sections.title: Hogs Crater, Cattle Split
sections.body: Lean hogs crashed through every support level Friday, closing $90.88 after an 8.6% meltdown that erased three weeks of gains in one session. **No clean catalyst**, just pure technical selling after the contract finally broke the $95 floor it had defended since March. Live cattle held relatively better at $248.90, down just a half percent, while feeders flipped the script at $367.38, up three-tenths on strength that showed up just when live cattle momentum faded. The **cattle complex is pricing different timelines** now: feeders looking at summer grass, live cattle dealing with current costs.
sections.bottom_line: Hogs broke hard, cattle complex split between feeder optimism and live reality.

sections.title: Grains End Week Mixed
sections.body: Corn closed the week at $4.56, down 11 cents Friday as the July contract gave back Thursday's gains on **technical selling**. Soybeans held better at $11.94, up four cents as the November contract rallied 16½ cents on **short covering ahead of China trade talks**. The carry's working in beans, new crop November trading above July for the first time since March, while corn's calendar spread stayed flat. Wheat closed $6.08, down 3¾ cents, marking time until the spring wheat planting window closes.
sections.catalyst: Soybeans bounce ahead of upcoming talks with China
sections.bottom_line: Beans found buyers on China talk optimism, corn gave back Thursday's technical gains.

sections.title: Energy Costs Stay Elevated
sections.body: WTI crude held $95.42 Friday, down just 30 cents despite **US strikes on Iranian tankers in the Gulf**. The Iran premium is baked in now, crude trading in a tight band even as Washington says peace talks continue. Diesel costs are **tempering cover crop adoption** in the Upper Midwest according to extension educators, the first real signal that elevated energy prices are changing planting decisions. Natural gas stayed weak at $2.76, the only energy input giving farmers relief.
sections.catalyst: US hits Iran-flagged oil tankers while peace talks continue
sections.bottom_line: Iran premium holding crude above $95, diesel costs changing planting decisions.

outside_the_pit.title: Court Rules Against Trump Global Tariff
outside_the_pit.body: The International Court of Trade ruled Thursday that Trump's 10% global tariff was unlawful under Section 122 trade law. The same court that struck down previous Trump tariffs is reshaping trade policy through the judiciary, not Congress.

outside_the_pit.title: Iowa Detects First Pseudorabies in 20 Years
outside_the_pit.body: First pseudorabies detection in a domestic commercial swine herd in over two decades hit Iowa this week. Industry officials say they were prepared, but any disease outbreak in concentrated hog production changes the risk calculus.

outside_the_pit.title: Armyworm Moths Flying North Early
outside_the_pit.body: Wisconsin entomologists report heavy true armyworm moth flights moving north this spring, earlier than normal seasonal patterns. Trap counts are already elevated, setting up potential crop pressure during peak growing season.

the_more_you_know.title: The 8.6% Rule: When Hogs Move This Hard, Listen
the_more_you_know.body: Friday's 8.6% hog crash wasn't random. Single-session moves above 8% in livestock futures happen maybe twice a year, and they're almost never about today's fundamentals. They're about positioning, about funds that got too comfortable in a range-bound market suddenly hitting exits at the same time. The last time hogs moved this hard was November, right before the contract found its real floor 15% lower. When livestock moves this violently without news, it's usually pricing something the calendar already shows.

watch_list.desc: Hogs above $93: Friday was capitulation, below: more to come
China trade talks: any soy purchase commitments change November carry
Feeder/live ratio: above 1.48 confirms the complex split

daily_quote.text: When you come to a fork in the road, take it.

source_summary: CME Group, USDA, Energy Information Administration, agricultural news services
